summary: Large item/Pokemon decompilation PR that replaced placeholder stubs with C implementations across many item files, plus the header/struct/prototype fixes needed to type item vars and special attributes. The PR context summary claims 17 matched functions across 11 item/Pokemon files; the decomp.dev bot reported +1852 matched code bytes, +64 matched data bytes, 12 new 100% matches, and several additional near-matches in item units.
what_changed: Implemented item/Pokemon functions previously left as `/// #...` placeholders, including Ice Climbers Ice Shot, Staryu/Hitodeman, Mewtwo Shadow Ball, Lugia, Blastoise/Kamex, Flipper, Weezing/Matadogas, Kirby Cutter Beam, and other item behaviors.

Changed `ftKb_AttackDashAir_800F21C0` from `void` to `float` and returned `ftPartGetRotX(GET_FIGHTER(gobj), 0)`, allowing `it_8029BB90` to use Kirby's attack angle for Final Cutter beam velocity and direction.

Expanded item-var and attribute structs in `itCommonItems.h` and `itCharItems.h`, replacing padding with typed fields such as `itKirbyCutterBeam_ItemVars`, `itHitodeman_ItemVars` floats, `itMewtwoShadowball_ItemVars` fields, Lugia vectors, Patapata attributes, and Climbers Ice attributes.

Implemented `it_802C16F8`, `itClimbersice_UnkMotion3_Phys`, and `it_2725_Logic90_HitShield` for Ice Shot launch, airborne physics/damage scaling, shield bounce velocity reduction, and lifetime threshold handling.
Implemented Hitodeman/Staryu initialization and velocity smoothing logic in `it_2725_Logic24_Spawned` and `it_802D48B0`.
Implemented `it_802C5B18` and related struct fixes for Mewtwo Shadow Ball launch/destruction state, with item vars split out of a prior integer array.
Implemented Lugia attack setup in `it_802D1A44`, including animation state, hitlag effect pause/resume callbacks, mouth/joint position sampling, beam target vectors, and accessory callback setup.
Implemented spawn/setup routines such as Flipper spawning from an `HSD_JObj` and Kamex/Blastoise spawned initialization with lifetime, animation, and model scale.
Added includes required by the new C, including `math.h`, `MSL/trigf.h`, `baselib/jobj.h`, `lb/lb_00B0.h`, and Kirby/item inline headers.
smart_moves: Unblocked item projectile code by fixing the upstream Kirby helper prototype instead of treating its return value as unknown; this made Kirby Cutter Beam angle computation expressible in typed C.
Converted anonymous padding into offset-annotated fields only where the new implementations needed real data, preserving layout while improving future readability.
Kept item vars named with offset-style placeholders (`x70`, `x8C`, `xDD4`, etc.) where semantics were still uncertain, avoiding overconfident names while enabling matching.
Matched low-level engine side effects explicitly: effect pause/resume callbacks, accessory callbacks, hitbox damage updates, item animation state changes, and JObj scale/rotation operations.
Used existing engine helpers rather than open-coding behavior, e.g. `Item_80268E5C`, `it_80275158`, `it_80272980`, `itColl_BounceOffVictim`, `efAsync_Spawn`, `lb_8000B1CC`, and `HSD_JObjSetScale`.
The bot report shows a useful split between exact new matches and near-matches, making it clear which functions were fully landed and which still need small ordering/register/data fixes.
decomp_lessons: Item decomp often requires header work first: struct field recovery in `xDD4_itemVar` and special-attribute structs can be the key to writing natural C that matches.
When a function appears to discard a call result, verify the prototype; here `ftKb_AttackDashAir_800F21C0` needed to return `float`, and the caller used that value for trig-based velocity.

For projectile setup functions, preserve both gameplay state and visual/effect state: velocity, facing direction, hitbox damage, lifetime, model scale, effect callbacks, and accessory callbacks can all affect matching.
Replacing padding with typed `Vec3` fields can simplify code and avoid casts, as shown by Lugia using `x70` as a vector instead of casting `x70_pad`.

Near-matches around 99% can still be valuable, but the bot output should be recorded precisely; this PR had 100% matches and several 97-99% improvements rather than all changed functions necessarily being perfect.
Evidence for human reviewer intent is weak in this slice: there were no review comments or reviews, only a decomp.dev bot match report.
naming_conventions: Preserved project-style unknown function names such as `it_802C16F8`, `it_802D48B0`, `it_2725_Logic31_Spawned`, and `it_8029BB90` when no semantic name was established.
Used existing item motion callback naming patterns such as `itClimbersice_UnkMotion3_Phys`, `itMewtwoshadowball_UnkMotion8_Anim`, and `itPatapata_UnkMotion4_Phys`.
Maintained item/Pokemon internal names used by the codebase: `hitodeman`, `kamex`, `matadogas`, `oldottosea`, `patapata`, `lugia`, and `mewtwoshadowball`.

Struct fields stayed offset-based (`x0`, `x40`, `x8C`, `xA4`, `xDD4`, `xE50`) unless semantics were clear enough for names such as `angle`, `speed`, `dir`, and `init_pos` in `itKirbyCutterBeam_ItemVars`.
Header comments preserve offset notation such as `/* +70 ip+E44 */` and `/* ip+DE4 */`, which is useful for future item-var layout recovery.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Used `PAD_STACK(24)` in `it_802C16F8`, indicating stack-shape control was needed for that match.
Used `ABS` and explicit sign handling in functions like `it_802D48B0` and Ice Shot shield logic to mirror original branch behavior.
Re-fetched `GET_ITEM(gobj)` and scoped locals in places such as Ice Shot code, a common tactic to influence register allocation and instruction order.
Called `cosf` and `sinf` from `MSL/trigf.h` for Kirby Cutter Beam velocity/direction, matching the original float trig calls.
Preserved inline JObj assertions and direct `jobj->rotate.y` mutation in `itMatadogas_UnkMotion2_Phys`, suggesting matching required the same baselib debug/assert pattern.
Used `lb_8000B1CC` to sample joint/world positions for Flipper spawning and Lugia beam setup, avoiding manual matrix math.
Updated `.sdata`/`.sdata2` match percentages in several item units according to the bot report, implying constants and small-data placement mattered alongside code matching.
review_feedback: No human review comments or review records were present in the slice.
The decomp.dev bot reported matched code improved to 61.15% (+0.05%, +1852 bytes) and matched data to 37.78% (+0.01%, +64 bytes).

Bot-listed 100% new matches included `itSeakneedlethrown_UnkMotion1_Anim`, `it_2725_Logic31_Spawned`, `it_802EBA00`, `it_802D1A44`, `itSeakneedlethrown_UnkMotion4_Anim`, `it_802C5B18`, `it_802D48B0`, and `itPatapata_UnkMotion4_Phys`, plus several `.sdata` matches.
follow_up_queries: Which near-matched functions from PR #2365 were later brought from 99% to 100%, especially `it_8029BB90`, `it_802C16F8`, and `it_2725_Logic90_HitShield`?
Search for later renames or semantic field recovery for `itKirbyCutterBeam_ItemVars`, `itHitodeman_ItemVars`, `itLugia_ItemVars`, and `itMewtwoShadowball_ItemVars`.
Check whether `ftKb_AttackDashAir_800F21C0` gained a semantic name after its return type was corrected to `float`.
Look for future PRs touching `.sdata2` constants in `itoldottosea`, `itmewtwoshadowball`, `itflipper`, `itkamex`, `ithitodeman`, or `ittincle`.
